items.name: GAF Commercial Roofing Systems in Springfield, MO
items.meta: GAF EverGuard TPO, PVC, and modified bitumen product lines explained for Springfield, MO commercial roof owners comparing manufacturer-specific bids.

sections.html:
GAF's Commercial Line: EverGuard and What Sits Behind the Name

GAF is a name most building owners already recognize from residential shingles, but their commercial roofing division is a separate product catalog entirely, covering single-ply membranes and modified bitumen systems relevant to the flat and low-slope roofs common on the buildings we work on around Springfield.

GAF's Position in the Commercial Market

GAF is one of the larger roofing manufacturers in North America by volume, and their commercial division, marketed under the EverGuard name, produces TPO and PVC membrane products alongside their long-standing modified bitumen line. Because GAF has scale advantages in raw material sourcing and distribution, their commercial products show up frequently in competitive bid comparisons, and it's worth knowing what's actually in the EverGuard catalog rather than treating GAF as a single undifferentiated product. That scale can be a genuine advantage on larger distribution and industrial jobs in this market, where material lead time and volume pricing matter more on a 100,000-square-foot roof than they would on a small retail strip reroof.

EverGuard TPO

GAF's TPO product line covers standard commercial mil thicknesses in both mechanically-attached and fully-adhered configurations. As with any TPO regardless of manufacturer, the mil thickness specified within the EverGuard line, rather than the brand name alone, is what determines puncture and hail impact resistance, which matters directly in a market with the hail exposure southwest Missouri sees most storm seasons.

EverGuard Extreme

GAF also markets a heavier-duty TPO product line under the EverGuard Extreme name, positioned for roofs needing higher puncture resistance or extended durability specifications. When a spec or bid references this specific product line rather than standard EverGuard TPO, it's worth confirming the actual mil thickness and reinforcement being proposed, since the Extreme branding alone doesn't specify the gauge.

  • EverGuard TPO membrane in standard commercial mil thicknesses
  • EverGuard Extreme heavier-duty TPO product line for high-traffic or high-exposure roofs
  • EverGuard PVC membrane for chemical and grease-resistant roof applications
  • Ruberoid modified bitumen product line, GAF's longstanding multi-ply system
  • StrataGrip and related base sheet products used within their modified bitumen assemblies

EverGuard PVC

GAF's PVC line addresses the same chemical and grease-resistance use case as any manufacturer's PVC product, and it comes into that conversation any time a mechanical engineer or health-department reviewer flags rooftop kitchen exhaust, industrial exposure, and chemical contact environments where TPO or EPDM would degrade prematurely. Like all heat-welded membrane, actual seam performance on GAF PVC depends on installer weld quality and probing discipline, not the manufacturer name alone.

Ruberoid Modified Bitumen

GAF's Ruberoid line is a long-established modified bitumen product family covering SBS-modified base and cap sheets in various application methods, including torch-applied and self-adhered options. This product line predates GAF's entry into single-ply membranes by decades and remains a common choice on roofs where multi-ply redundancy is the design priority over single-sheet membrane systems. On a hail-exposed roof where redundancy matters more than the lighter installed weight of a single-ply system, Ruberoid stays in the conversation specifically because of that layered construction, base sheet and cap sheet each contributing separate waterproofing, rather than relying on one continuous membrane to do the entire job.

Reading a GAF-Specified Bid Correctly

A proposal that simply lists GAF TPO without naming EverGuard versus EverGuard Extreme, or without stating mil thickness, isn't giving you enough information to compare against a competing bid on equal footing. We name the specific product line and thickness whenever a manufacturer is specified, so the comparison between bids is actually apples to apples rather than brand name versus brand name.

Warranty and System Compatibility

Like most major manufacturers, GAF's warranty programs are generally tied to using matched components across the assembly, meaning membrane, insulation, and fasteners from their approved product list, installed per their specifications. Substituting a non-GAF insulation board to save cost on a project intended to carry a GAF warranty can jeopardize that warranty even if the membrane itself is genuine, which is a detail worth confirming before signing any manufacturer-warrantied project. We spell out every component in the assembly, membrane, insulation, cover board, fasteners, by name and manufacturer in our proposals, so an owner can verify the full system matches what the warranty actually requires before work starts, not after.

Questions on GAF Commercial Roofing Products

What's the difference between EverGuard and EverGuard Extreme?

Extreme is positioned as a heavier-duty product line within the same TPO family, but the specific mil thickness and reinforcement should be confirmed directly rather than assumed from the product name.

Is GAF's modified bitumen line still commonly used on new roofs?

Yes, Ruberoid modified bitumen remains a specified option, particularly where multi-ply redundancy is prioritized over single-ply membrane systems.

Does GAF PVC perform differently than other manufacturers' PVC?

Core PVC performance characteristics are similar across manufacturers at comparable thickness; installation quality, particularly seam welding and thorough probing after every run, is generally the larger factor in long-term performance.

Can GAF TPO be mixed with another manufacturer's insulation?

It can be installed that way, but doing so may affect eligibility for a GAF system warranty, which typically requires matched, approved components across the full roof assembly.

Is EverGuard TPO rated for high hail exposure?

Impact resistance depends on the specific mil thickness within the EverGuard line selected for the project, which we size to the building's actual hail exposure and roof size rather than defaulting to whichever base-tier product keeps a bid the cheapest on paper.

items.name: Elevate (Firestone) Roofing Systems in Springfield, MO
items.meta: Elevate, formerly Firestone Building Products, TPO and EPDM product lines explained for Springfield, MO owners comparing manufacturer-specific roof bids.

sections.html:
Elevate: The Name Change Behind Firestone's Roofing Products

Building owners who've had a roof replaced in the last decade or two may recognize the Firestone name on their old warranty paperwork and wonder why a new bid references a name they've never heard of. Elevate is that same manufacturer's commercial roofing product line under new branding, and the underlying product families are largely continuous with what was previously sold as Firestone Building Products.

The Firestone-to-Elevate Transition

Firestone Building Products' commercial roofing division was renamed Elevate as part of a broader corporate rebranding under its parent company. The product lines that made Firestone a recognizable name in commercial single-ply roofing, particularly their EPDM products, continued under Elevate's branding rather than being discontinued. If you have an existing Firestone-branded roof and are getting a bid for repair, recover, or replacement work, it's worth knowing the current manufacturer name in case warranty documentation or product-matching becomes relevant. This kind of rebrand trips up building owners more often than it should, because a contractor pulling permits or ordering repair material under the old name can end up sourcing a product that no longer matches current formulations, even when the intent was simply to patch an existing roof with compatible material.

RubberGard EPDM on Ozarks Rooftops

RubberGard is the long-standing EPDM membrane product line carried forward from Firestone into Elevate's current catalog. Given EPDM's flexibility advantage through repeated freeze-thaw cycling, this product line remains relevant to buildings in this market where that seasonal temperature swing is a real design factor, provided the system specified uses factory seam tape rather than older liquid-adhesive seaming methods. Older RubberGard installations from decades back often used liquid-adhesive seams, since factory tape wasn't standard practice industry-wide at the time, so a roof with a Firestone RubberGard history is worth a closer seam inspection rather than an assumption that it was built to current seam standards.

UltraPly TPO Under Missouri Hail Exposure

Elevate's TPO product line is marketed under the UltraPly name, covering standard commercial mil thicknesses for mechanically-attached and fully-adhered TPO systems. As with any TPO product regardless of manufacturer, the specific mil thickness within this line, not the brand name alone, determines the membrane's actual impact and puncture resistance for hail exposure common to this region. We size UltraPly the same way we size any manufacturer's TPO, matching gauge to the building's hail history and roof size rather than defaulting to the lightest available option to keep a bid competitive on price alone.

  • RubberGard EPDM membrane, the product line carried forward from Firestone
  • UltraPly TPO membrane in standard commercial mil thicknesses
  • Insulation and cover board products manufactured to pair with their membrane systems
  • Fastening and adhesive components specified as part of matched system assemblies
  • Modified bitumen products offered within their broader commercial catalog

Why the Rebrand Matters for Existing Roof Owners

If your building has an existing Firestone RubberGard or UltraPly roof, repairs or partial recover work should generally use compatible current-generation Elevate products to maintain material compatibility and any active warranty coverage. A contractor unfamiliar with the Firestone-to-Elevate transition might not realize an older warranty is still traceable to the current manufacturer, which can matter if a warranty claim becomes relevant. We ask for whatever original paperwork an owner still has, even a partial invoice or product label photo from the original install, before quoting repair work on an older membrane, because that history determines which current-line product is actually compatible, and getting it wrong can leave two incompatible membrane generations spliced together on the same roof.

Reading an Elevate-Specified Bid Correctly

Similar to any manufacturer-specific proposal, naming Elevate EPDM or Elevate TPO alone isn't enough detail to compare against a competing bid. The specific product line, mil thickness, and attachment method within Elevate's catalog need to be named for an apples-to-apples comparison. We include that level of detail in any proposal referencing a specific manufacturer's product.

Warranty Considerations on Rebranded Products

Warranty terms and coverage structures can shift during a corporate rebrand even when the underlying product formulation stays largely consistent, so it's worth confirming current warranty terms directly with documentation rather than assuming older Firestone warranty language still applies word for word under the Elevate name. We treat this as a standard checklist item on any repair quote for an older RubberGard or UltraPly roof, since skipping it risks doing work that inadvertently voids coverage the owner assumed was still active.

Questions on Elevate Roofing Products

Is Elevate the same company as Firestone Building Products?

Elevate is the current branding for what was previously marketed as Firestone Building Products' commercial roofing division, with continuity in core product lines like RubberGard EPDM.

Can I get warranty service on an old Firestone roof through Elevate?

In many cases yes, since the product lines and manufacturer entity are continuous, but this should be confirmed with actual warranty documentation for the specific roof.

Is RubberGard EPDM still a commonly specified product?

Yes, it remains one of the established EPDM product lines in the commercial market, particularly relevant where EPDM's flexibility characteristics fit the building's needs.

Does UltraPly TPO come in different mil thicknesses for hail-prone regions?

Yes, like other manufacturers' TPO lines, mil thickness varies within the product family and should be matched to the building's actual hail exposure.

Should I mention the Firestone name when getting a bid for repair work on an older roof?

Yes, naming the original manufacturer and product line helps ensure any repair materials are compatible with the existing roof system.

items.name: Johns Manville Roofing Systems in Springfield, MO
items.meta: Johns Manville TPO and DynaWeld modified bitumen product lines explained for Springfield, MO commercial building owners comparing manufacturer specs.

sections.html:
Johns Manville: A Materials Manufacturer With Deep Modified Bitumen Roots

Johns Manville is a name with a long history in building materials broadly, beyond roofing alone, and their commercial roofing division carries product lines spanning both modern single-ply membrane and traditional modified bitumen systems. Understanding which line is being specified in a bid tells you a lot about what kind of roof you're actually being sold, and how that roof is likely to hold up against the hail, wind, and freeze-thaw cycling that define this market's storm calendar.

Johns Manville's Position in the Market

Johns Manville is a Denver-based manufacturer with a long operating history in insulation and building materials, and its roofing division produces both TPO single-ply membrane and modified bitumen products under long-established brand names within its catalog. Because the company's roots are heavily in built-up and modified bitumen roofing, their product depth in multi-ply systems tends to be a differentiator compared to manufacturers that came to the roofing market primarily through single-ply membrane.

DynaWeld and DynaBase Modified Bitumen

Johns Manville's DynaWeld and DynaBase product names cover their SBS-modified bitumen base and cap sheet systems, used in torch-applied, self-adhered, and other application methods relevant to multi-ply roof assemblies. For a building owner considering modified bitumen specifically for its redundancy advantage over single-ply, understanding that these are named, distinct product lines rather than a generic modified bitumen label helps in comparing bids that reference Johns Manville by name. The base sheet and cap sheet work as two separate waterproofing layers, so a flaw in one ply doesn't automatically mean a leak the way a puncture through a single-sheet membrane does, which is the core case for choosing a multi-ply system on a roof with heavy hail exposure. SBS modification specifically means the asphalt is blended with a rubber polymer that stays flexible at low temperatures, a real factor given how often the Ozarks region crosses the freezing point during a typical winter instead of holding one steady cold stretch.

JM TPO

Johns Manville also manufactures TPO membrane products in standard commercial mil thicknesses for mechanically-attached and adhered applications. As with any TPO regardless of manufacturer, the actual gauge specified within their product line is what determines puncture and hail impact resistance, which is the detail worth confirming on any bid rather than assuming from the brand name alone.

  • DynaWeld and DynaBase SBS modified bitumen base and cap sheet systems
  • JM TPO membrane in standard commercial mil thicknesses
  • Invinsa structural roof panel products for specific application categories
  • Insulation and cover board products manufactured to pair with their roofing systems
  • Fastening components and accessories specified as part of matched assemblies

Invinsa Panels

Johns Manville also produces Invinsa, a fiber-reinforced structural panel product used in certain roof deck and reroofing applications where the existing deck needs additional structural contribution as part of the roof assembly. This is a more specialized product than field membrane and typically comes into play on specific structural or recover scenarios rather than as a general-purpose roofing material. It's worth raising during any inspection of an older building where the roof deck itself shows signs of deterioration, since a structural panel product addresses a different problem than membrane replacement alone and can change the scope and cost of a project significantly.

Why Multi-Ply Depth Matters for This Market

Given southwest Missouri's hail exposure and the redundancy advantage of multi-ply systems over single-sheet membrane, a manufacturer with genuine depth in modified bitumen product engineering, rather than a single-ply company that added a modified bitumen line as an afterthought, is worth knowing when a bid specifically proposes a multi-ply system for hail resilience.

Reading a Johns Manville-Specified Bid Correctly

As with any manufacturer, naming Johns Manville modified bitumen alone doesn't tell you ply count, application method, or cap sheet granule specification. We name the specific DynaWeld or DynaBase product, ply count, and application method explicitly in any proposal referencing this manufacturer, so the comparison against a competing bid is meaningful.

Warranty and Assembly Matching

Like other major manufacturers, Johns Manville's warranty coverage is generally tied to using matched, approved components across the full assembly. Confirming which specific warranty tier and component list applies to a proposed project is worth doing directly rather than assuming a generic warranty applies regardless of what's actually installed. This matters more than it sounds like it should, because a crew that substitutes a cheaper insulation board or a non-matched fastener to shave cost off a bid can quietly void warranty eligibility on a system that was sold to the owner as fully covered, and that gap usually doesn't surface until a claim gets filed years later.

Questions on Johns Manville Roofing Products

Is Johns Manville primarily known for modified bitumen or single-ply membrane?

The company has particularly deep roots in modified bitumen and built-up roofing systems, alongside a more modern TPO single-ply product line.

What's the difference between DynaWeld and DynaBase?

These are distinct named products within their modified bitumen line, typically differentiated by application method or layer function within the assembly, which should be confirmed for the specific system being proposed.

Is JM TPO comparable to other major manufacturers' TPO?

Core TPO performance characteristics are broadly similar across major manufacturers at comparable mil thickness; installation quality is generally the larger factor in long-term performance.

When would Invinsa panels be relevant to a reroofing project?

They come into play in specific structural or recover scenarios where the existing deck needs additional structural contribution, which is a site-specific engineering determination.

Does a multi-ply modified bitumen system from Johns Manville cost more than single-ply TPO?

Typically yes, due to the additional material layers and labor involved, though the redundancy benefit is the tradeoff being purchased with that added cost.

items.name: Sika Sarnafil Roofing Systems in Springfield, MO
items.meta: Sika Sarnafil PVC membrane product lines explained for Springfield, MO commercial building owners comparing chemical resistance and welded-seam specs.

sections.html:
Sarnafil: A PVC-First Manufacturer, Not a TPO Company With a PVC Line

Some manufacturers came to PVC as one product among many. Sarnafil built its reputation specifically around PVC membrane, which is worth knowing when a bid or spec references this name specifically rather than a generalist single-ply manufacturer.

Sika Sarnafil's Background

Sarnafil is a PVC roofing membrane product line with a long operating history that predates much of the current single-ply membrane market, now owned by Sika, a global building materials company headquartered in Switzerland. Because Sarnafil's product development has centered on PVC membrane specifically for decades rather than treating it as a secondary product line, their catalog reflects a depth of PVC-specific formulation work that's a genuine differentiator among membrane manufacturers.

Core PVC Membrane Products

Sarnafil's flagship membrane products cover standard commercial mil thicknesses for heat-welded PVC roofing, used in the same chemical and grease-resistant applications as any manufacturer's PVC line: rooftop kitchen exhaust, food processing exposure, and industrial chemical environments where TPO or EPDM would degrade prematurely. As with all PVC regardless of manufacturer, seam quality at install, meaning weld temperature and probing discipline, is the primary determinant of long-term seam performance. Impact resistance under hail follows the same rule as any welded membrane: thickness and reinforcement drive puncture resistance, not the brand name on the roll, which is why we still walk through mil thickness selection against the building's actual exposure rather than treating a recognizable manufacturer as a substitute for that conversation.

Reinforced and Fleece-Backed Options

Sarnafil offers reinforced membrane variants intended for use in fully-adhered assemblies over rigid insulation, similar in concept to fleece-backed products from other manufacturers, providing additional puncture resistance from the underside of the membrane. This is a relevant option on recover projects where an existing roof surface has some irregularity that a standard mechanically-attached sheet wouldn't accommodate as cleanly. On older buildings around this market where an existing built-up or modified bitumen roof has settled unevenly over the years, a fleece-backed sheet can bridge minor surface irregularities that would otherwise telegraph through a thinner, unreinforced membrane.

  • Heat-welded PVC membrane in standard commercial mil thicknesses
  • Reinforced and fleece-backed PVC variants for fully-adhered assemblies
  • Wide color range within their PVC line, beyond the standard white and gray options
  • Chemical-resistant formulation options for specific industrial exposure conditions
  • Accessory and flashing membrane matched to field sheet chemical resistance grade

Why PVC Specialization Matters on the Right Building

On a building with rooftop kitchen exhaust, chemical storage venting, or industrial process exposure, the actual chemical resistance formulation of the PVC sheet matters considerably more than it does on a standard office or retail roof. A manufacturer whose core business has been PVC-specific formulation for a long operating history is a reasonable place to look when that specific exposure profile is driving the membrane decision, though the same seam-quality and installation caveats that apply to any PVC product still apply here.

Color and Aesthetic Options

Sarnafil's PVC line is also known for offering a broader color range than the standard white or gray typical of most commercial membrane, which occasionally matters on buildings with visible rooftop areas from adjacent structures or where an owner has a specific aesthetic requirement. This is a secondary consideration relative to performance specification, but worth mentioning since it does distinguish this product line from most competitors. We still evaluate thickness and reinforcement first on any project where color is part of the conversation, since an appealing color option on an under-specified membrane doesn't change what the roof needs to survive a Missouri hailstorm, and we'd rather an owner pick color last, after the performance spec is locked in, than the other way around.

Reading a Sarnafil-Specified Bid Correctly

A proposal naming Sarnafil should still specify the exact product line, mil thickness, and whether a standard or reinforced membrane is being proposed, the same due diligence that applies to any manufacturer-named bid. We include that detail explicitly rather than letting a recognizable manufacturer name substitute for actual specification clarity. A well-known brand on a proposal isn't a substitute for a scope breakdown, and we've seen bids elsewhere in this market lean on manufacturer reputation to skip over exactly the details, thickness, attachment method, flashing spec, that determine how the roof actually performs.

Warranty Structure

As with other major manufacturers, Sarnafil's warranty coverage is generally tied to using matched, approved system components installed per their published specifications, which is worth confirming directly for any project where warranty terms factor into the decision.

Questions on Sika Sarnafil Products

Is Sarnafil PVC different from other manufacturers' PVC membrane?

The core chemistry category is similar, but Sarnafil's long specialization in PVC-specific formulation is a relevant factor for buildings with demanding chemical exposure requirements.

Does Sarnafil PVC cost more than a generalist manufacturer's PVC line?

Pricing varies by market conditions and specific product selected, so this should be compared directly on the specific project rather than assumed.

Is reinforced Sarnafil PVC necessary for a standard recover project?

Not always. It depends on the existing roof surface condition and whether additional underside puncture resistance is warranted for that specific substrate.

Can Sarnafil PVC be specified in colors other than white?

Yes, their product line includes a broader color range than most standard commercial membrane offerings.

Does PVC from any manufacturer resist Springfield hail as well as thicker TPO?

Impact resistance depends primarily on membrane thickness within the specific product line selected, which we size to the building's actual hail exposure rather than a default gauge.

sections.html:
Versico: A Second Membrane Brand Under the Same Corporate Roof as Carlisle

Versico is a name that sometimes confuses building owners who are also seeing Carlisle SynTec quoted elsewhere in the bidding process, since both brands are part of the same parent company, Carlisle Companies, while operating as distinct product lines with their own manufacturing and distribution.

Versico's Position in the Market

Versico Roofing Systems is a commercial roofing membrane manufacturer based in Carlisle, Pennsylvania, operating as a subsidiary within the Carlisle Companies portfolio alongside Carlisle SynTec. The two brands maintain separate product identities and distribution channels even though they share corporate ownership, so a bid referencing Versico is referencing a distinct product catalog, not simply a relabeled Carlisle product.

VersiWeld TPO in the Hail Belt

Versico's TPO product line is marketed under the VersiWeld name, covering standard commercial mil thicknesses for mechanically-attached and fully-adhered TPO systems. As with any TPO regardless of manufacturer, mil thickness within this specific product line, not the brand name, is what determines the membrane's puncture and hail impact resistance relevant to this market's storm exposure. Fastening pattern and attachment method still need to be sized to the specific building's wind zone and roof height, since a mismatched fastening density can undercut even a well-rated membrane's wind uplift performance regardless of which manufacturer supplied the sheet.

VersiGard EPDM for Springfield Buildings

Versico also manufactures EPDM membrane under the VersiGard name, in both black and white surface options and in the mil thicknesses relevant to standard commercial applications. The same seam-method consideration that applies to any EPDM product applies here: factory seam-taped systems perform more reliably through the Ozarks' repeated freeze-thaw cycling than older liquid-adhesive seaming methods. Black VersiGard suits unconditioned warehouse space where the added winter heat gain can offset heating cost, while the white option is worth weighing on conditioned office or medical buildings where summer cooling load is the bigger factor.

  • VersiWeld TPO membrane in standard commercial mil thicknesses
  • VersiGard EPDM membrane in black and white surface options
  • VersiFlex PVC membrane products for chemical and grease-resistant applications
  • Fleece-backed membrane options for fully-adhered recover assemblies
  • Insulation and cover board products manufactured to pair with their membrane lines

VersiFlex PVC Where Chemistry Matters

Versico's PVC line, marketed as VersiFlex, addresses the same chemical and grease-resistance use case as any manufacturer's PVC product: rooftop kitchen exhaust, food service exposure, and industrial chemical contact where TPO or EPDM would degrade prematurely. Seam performance on this product depends on the same weld-quality and probing discipline that applies to any heat-welded PVC membrane, which we hold to the identical probing standard we use on TPO and standard PVC jobs, regardless of which manufacturer's sheet is on the roof.

Why the Carlisle-Versico Relationship Comes Up in Bidding

Because both brands sit under the same corporate parent, some contractors carry certification or distribution relationships with one brand but not the other, which can influence which manufacturer shows up in a given bid regardless of whether one product is actually better suited to the building than the other. It's worth asking directly why a specific brand was chosen for a project rather than assuming the choice reflects a technical evaluation of the building's needs. That question isn't a knock on either brand, since both manufacture genuinely comparable membrane products, but an owner comparing two bids that reference different brands under the same parent company should know they're not necessarily looking at two independently engineered systems. We'll quote whichever brand fits the project best, and we'll say so plainly if two competing bids referencing Carlisle and Versico both land on essentially the same membrane spec dressed up under different names.

Reading a Versico-Specified Bid Correctly

As with any manufacturer, a bid that simply states VersiWeld TPO without naming mil thickness and attachment method isn't giving enough detail to compare fairly against a competing proposal. We name the specific product line, gauge, and attachment method explicitly whenever a manufacturer is specified.

Warranty Considerations

Versico's warranty programs, like other major manufacturers, are generally structured around matched, approved system components installed to their specifications. Confirming the specific warranty tier and required component list is worth doing directly for any project where warranty coverage factors into the material decision, particularly on a larger distribution or industrial building where the cost of a denied claim years down the road would be substantial. A substituted fastener, an unapproved insulation swap, or a flashing detail that deviates from the manufacturer's published assembly can each independently affect eligibility, even when the field membrane itself is a genuine Versico product installed by a competent crew.

Questions on Versico Roofing Products

Is Versico the same as Carlisle SynTec?

They share a corporate parent, Carlisle Companies, but operate as separate product brands with their own catalogs and distribution.

Is VersiWeld TPO a lower-cost alternative to Carlisle Sure-Weld TPO?

Pricing varies by market conditions and specific product selected rather than one brand being categorically cheaper, so this should be compared directly on the specific project.

Does VersiGard EPDM use seam tape or liquid adhesive?

Both seaming methods exist across the EPDM product category broadly; we specify factory seam-taped systems regardless of which manufacturer's EPDM is used.

Can Versico products be mixed with another manufacturer's insulation?

They can be installed that way, but doing so may affect eligibility for a Versico system warranty, which typically requires matched, approved components.

Is VersiFlex PVC suitable for a restaurant rooftop exhaust environment?

Yes, PVC as a membrane category is generally suited to grease and chemical exposure, consistent with the performance characteristics of PVC products broadly.
